chunk_summary: This section outlines baseline comorbidity screening and escalation checkpoints for tele-obesity care in pediatric populations in India, emphasizing accurate measurements, risk stratification, and clear escalation criteria for in-person care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of baseline comorbidity screening in tele-nutrition pathways for pediatric obesity.
Learn to operationalize a minimum baseline dataset for tele-obesity care, including anthropometric and clinical parameters.
Identify escalation triggers requiring in-person assessment in pediatric tele-obesity care.
Apply WHO and IAP growth standards for accurate pediatric BMI classification.
key_facts: WHO standards are used for children under 5 years, and IAP charts are used for ages 5–18 years for BMI classification.
Minimum baseline dataset includes height, weight, BMI, blood pressure, waist circumference, pubertal context, sleep screen, and psychosocial screen.
Baseline labs for children ≥10 years with obesity include fasting lipids, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and ALT.
Escalation triggers include symptoms of diabetes, suspected hypertension, sleep-disordered breathing, rapid weight gain with systemic symptoms, or positive eating disorder risk screen.
Implementation checkpoints are set at 4–6 weeks for behavior changes and 12 weeks for trajectory assessment.
reference_quote: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Treatment of Children and Adolescents With Obesity (AAP, 2023): 'Classification must use pediatric references, not adult cutoffs.'
document_key_topics: Baseline comorbidity screening in tele-obesity care
Escalation criteria for pediatric tele-nutrition pathways
Use of WHO and IAP growth standards for BMI classification
